titleStmt.title: Letter from Wilfrid Spender to Lillian Spender, 8 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland
msIdentifier.collection: Letters from Sir Wilfred Spender to Lady Spender, D1633/1/1/49

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Nov. 8. My Just a buff slip today
to thank you for your last daily
letter an extra nice one, &
to give the pink pares something
to find in the bran pie enclosure. Yesterday was quite interesting
& if the Goc now wishes me
to go I shall be surprised, as
he must have seen by the
way the others treated me
that he has not got a yonny
cub of a captain. I too find a pr r is just
as bothering as ever & that
I miss her evidently more
than Miss L Dean misses
her b w sometimes! The GOC has ordered me to
return to the House of Lords via
the Duke of Luynes who has
as have also all the other Liaison
officers — not the interpreters of course —
because so on dit they talked too
much. I showed that I was
not too willing to come back
as the family party in the
Commons with Borjian Smith
is certainly a cheerier crew, &
I let them know that I didn't
wish to be a kind of gormers
to dim in to make up the party. My chilblained right
hand is itching so that I
can't go on writing. I am
glad I left off rings. Are you
wearing mine? Did you write
to the Dean & if so what was his
answer? A poor snubbed p r ? I believe you are staying at Rugby
but dare not risk it & any how you
will be more definite next time. Your BW
